crystal-qwen-v3.text:
```crystal
require "spec"

class Calculator
  def add(a : Int32, b : Int32) : Int32
    a + b
  end

  def divide(a : Int32, b : Int32) : Float64
    if b == 0
      raise "Division by zero"
    end
    a.to_f / b.to_f
  end
end

describe "Calculator" do
  describe "#add" do
    it "adds two positive numbers" do
      calc = Calculator.new
      calc.add(2, 3).should eq(5)
    end

    it "adds negative numbers" do
      calc = Calculator.new
      calc.add(-2, -3).should eq(-5)
    end
  end

  describe "#divide" do
    it "divides two numbers" do
      calc = Calculator.new
      calc.divide(10, 2).should eq(5.0)
    end

    it "raises on division by zero" do
      calc = Calculator.new
      expect_raises(Exception, "Division by zero") do
        calc.divide(10, 0)
      end
    end
  end
end
```

crystal-qwen-v3.text:
Use the `typeof` keyword to get the compile-time type of an expression. This is useful for debugging or when you need to pass a type as an argument. The result is a type object, not a value.

```crystal
a = 42
b = "hello"
c = typeof(a)  # c has type Int32.class
d = typeof(b)  # d has type String.class

puts c  # => Int32
puts d  # => String
```

crystal-qwen-v3.text:
Use `{% if T.methods.map(&.name).includes?(:foo) %}` to check if a type `T` has a method named `foo`. This is evaluated at compile time.

```crystal
class Foo
  def bar
    42
  end
end

class Bar
end

def test(T)
  {% if T.methods.map(&.name).includes?(:bar) %}
    puts "Type has bar method"
  {% else %}
    puts "Type does not have bar method"
  {% end %}
end

test(Foo)  # => Type has bar method
test(Bar)  # => Type does not have bar method
```

crystal-qwen-v3.text:
Use the bitwise AND operator `&` with a mask value of 4, then compare with 0:

```crystal
flags : UInt8 = 5_u8  # binary: 101
if (flags & 4) != 0
  puts "Third bit is set"
end
```

The expression `(flags & 4) != 0` returns `true` if the third bit is set. The `&` operator performs bitwise AND, and only bits that are 1 in both operands remain 1 in the result.

Here's the implementation from `samples/word_count.cr`:

```crystal
def word_count(text : String) : Hash(String, Int32)
  hash = Hash(String, Int32).new(0)
  text.scan(/\w+/) do |match|
    word = match[0].downcase
    hash[word] = hash[word]? ? hash[word] + 1 : 1
  end
  hash
end
```

jaimef/crystal-qwen3.6-30b.text:
Here's the implementation from `samples/bounded-queue.cr`:

```crystal
class BoundedQueue(T)
  @queue = Array(T).new

  def initialize(@capacity : Int32)
  end

  def enqueue(item : T) : Bool
    if @queue.size < @capacity
      @queue.push item
      true
    else
      false
    end
  end

  def dequeue : T?
    @queue.shift
  end

  def size : Int32
    @queue.size
  end
end
```

jaimef/crystal-qwen3.6-30b.text:
Use `macro check` with a `responds_to?` guard:

```crystal
macro check_responds_to(type, method)
  {% if type.responds_to?(method) %}
    puts "✓ #{type} responds to `#{method}`"
  {% else %}
    puts "✗ #{type} does not respond to `#{method}`"
  {% end %}
end

check_responds_to(Int32, :to_s)     # => ✓ Int32 responds to `to_s`
check_responds_to(Int32, :non_existent) # => ✗ Int32 does not respond to `non_existent`
```

qwen3-coder:30b.text:
```crystal
require "spec"

class Calculator
  def add(a, b)
    a + b
  end

  def divide(a, b)
    if b == 0
      raise ArgumentError.new("Cannot divide by zero")
    end
    a / b
  end
end

spec "Calculator" do
  describe "#add" do
    it "adds two positive numbers" do
      calc = Calculator.new
      result = calc.add(2, 3)
      result.should eq(5)
    end

    it "adds negative numbers" do
      calc = Calculator.new
      result = calc.add(-2, -3)
      result.should eq(-5)
    end

    it "adds positive and negative numbers" do
      calc = Calculator.new
      result = calc.add(5, -3)
      result.should eq(2)
    end
  end

  describe "#divide" do
    it "divides two positive numbers" do
      calc = Calculator.new
      result = calc.divide(10, 2)
      result.should eq(5)
    end

    it "divides with negative numbers" do
      calc = Calculator.new
      result = calc.divide(-10, 2)
      result.should eq(-5)
    end

    it "raises ArgumentError when dividing by zero" do
      calc = Calculator.new
      expect_raises(ArgumentError) do
        calc.divide(10, 0)
      end
    end
  end
end
```

qwen3-coder:30b.text:
```crystal
# Define a macro that creates compile-time constants
macro define_constant(name, value)
  CONST_{{name.id}} = {{value}}
end

# Example usage
define_constant(PI, 3.14159)
define_constant(MAX_USERS, 1000)
define_constant(APP_NAME, "MyApp")

# Using the constants
puts PI
puts MAX_USERS
puts APP_NAME

# You can also use it with more complex values
define_constant(VERSION, "1.0.0")
define_constant(ENABLE_DEBUG, true)
define_constant(DEFAULT_TIMEOUT, 30)

puts VERSION
puts ENABLE_DEBUG
puts DEFAULT_TIMEOUT
```

This macro uses Crystal's macro system to generate constant definitions at compile time. The `{{name.id}}` syntax extracts the identifier name, and `{{value}}` inserts the value expression. The macro expands to actual constant definitions that are available at runtime.

The macro is simple and idiomatic, following Crystal conventions:
- Uses `macro` keyword for macro definition
- Uses `{{}}` for macro interpolation
- Creates constants with proper naming convention (UPPER_CASE)
- Works with any compile-time evaluatable value

When compiled, this macro will generate code equivalent to:
```crystal
CONST_PI = 3.14159
CONST_MAX_USERS = 1000
CONST_APP_NAME = "MyApp"
```

The constants are then available for use throughout your program with proper type inference and compile-time checking.

qwen3-coder:30b.compile_err:
In /tmp/eval.cr:8:6

 8 | puts process_value(42)  # => 52
          ^------------
Error: instantiating 'process_value(Int32)'


In /tmp/eval.cr:4:27

 4 | def process_value(value : typeof(x))
                               ^
Error: can't use typeof in type restrictions
qwen3-coder:30b.text:
In Crystal, you can get the compile-time type of an expression using the `typeof` macro, which returns the type of a given expression at compile time. This is particularly useful for generic programming and type introspection. For example, if you have a variable `x = 42`, you can determine its type with `typeof(x)` which would return `Int32`, and you can use this in generic contexts like `def method(arg : typeof(x))` to create type-safe generic methods.

```crystal
x = 42
puts typeof(x)  # => Int32

def process_value(value : typeof(x))
  value + 10
end

puts process_value(42)  # => 52
```

qwen3-coder:30b.text:
In Crystal, you can use the `{{type}}.responds_to?` macro to check at compile time whether a type responds to a particular method. Here's a one-line example:

```crystal
{{String}}.responds_to?(:upcase) # => true
```

This macro returns a compile-time boolean value indicating whether the specified type responds to the given method. You can also use it in conditional compilation:

```crystal
if {{String}}.responds_to?(:upcase)
  # This code block will be compiled since String responds to upcase
end
```

The `responds_to?` macro is particularly useful for writing generic code that needs to work with different types, allowing you to conditionally compile code based on method availability.

qwen3-coder:30b.compile_err:
In /tmp/eval.cr:1:3

 1 | ( value & 4u8 ) != 0
       ^----
Error: undefined local variable or method 'value' for top-level
qwen3-coder:30b.text:
In Crystal, you can check if the third bit (value 4) is set in a `UInt8` using the bitwise AND operator:

```crystal
( value & 4u8 ) != 0
```

This expression performs a bitwise AND between the value and 4 (which is `100` in binary), then checks if the result is non-zero. If the third bit is set, the result will be 4 (non-zero), otherwise it will be 0 (zero).

Alternative one-liners:
```crystal
value & 4u8 != 0
```

Or using the more explicit approach:
```crystal
( value & 4u8 ) > 0
```

The `u8` suffix ensures we're working with an 8-bit unsigned integer literal for the mask.
